metadata.summary: Bun is a JavaScript/TypeScript runtime, bundler, test runner, and package manager in a single binary with measurably faster startup, install, and test speed than Node equivalents.

content.title: Bun Runtime
content.summary: Bun is a JavaScript/TypeScript runtime, bundler, test runner, and package manager in a single binary. It runs TypeScript directly without transpilation, provides native HTTP via Bun.serve, and replaces the npm + tsx + jest + esbuild stack. Startup latency, install time, and test speed are all significantly faster than Node equivalents. Default framework pairing is Hono; database ORM is Drizzle with bun:sqlite.

why.p: Startup latency, install time, and test speed are all significantly faster than Node equivalents. Eliminating the ts-node/tsx transpilation layer removes a common misconfiguration surface. The "one binary" model simplifies CI and reduces agent tooling errors. Bun.serve and Bun.file are measurably faster than Node counterparts for I/O-heavy routes.

Bun runs TypeScript directly without transpilation, provides native HTTP via Bun.serve, and replaces the npm + tsx + jest + esbuild stack with a single coherent tool. Default framework pairing is Hono; database ORM is Drizzle with bun:sqlite. All three are tightly integrated and battle-tested.

list.item: Greenfield TypeScript backends where cold-start latency or CI install speed matters.
CLI tools and build scripts that need fast execution.
Hono / Elysia API servers targeting Bun.serve natively.
Replacing npm + tsx + jest stack with a single dependency.
Monorepos with frequent install cycles — Bun install is 10-30x faster.

Long-running production Node.js services already battle-tested — Bun's Node compat is strong (>95%) but not 100%; surprising failures in node:cluster, worker_threads, native modules with node-gyp. Don't migrate stable infra without a parallel test rig.
Apps depending on niche npm packages with native bindings (some Postgres / image libs) — Bun's NAPI is improving but not flawless.
Edge providers (Vercel, Cloudflare Workers) that don't support Bun runtime — your app runs on V8, not Bun.
Teams wedded to Jest's ecosystem (snapshot tooling, transformers) — bun test is fast and Jest-compatible-ish, but plugin ecosystem is thin.
Windows-first dev teams — Bun on Windows works (since 2024) but lags macOS/Linux.

section.name: initialization

rule.statement: Pin the Bun version in package.json packageManager field and in CI (oven-sh/setup-bun@v2 with bun-version). Never use "latest".
rule.rationale: Reproducibility requires identical Bun versions across developer machines, CI, and production containers. LLMs default to "latest" which breaks builds across teams.

section.p: Initialize a new Bun project with bun init, which scaffolds a minimal package.json. Add dependencies for the intended runtime and framework — Hono for API servers, Elysia as an alternative, Drizzle for database access.

example.title: Bun project initialization
example.code: bun init bun add hono zod bun add -d @types/bun

rule.statement: Define dev, start, test, and build scripts in package.json. Use bun --watch for dev (full restart) not bun --hot (persists state).
rule.rationale: --hot keeps top-level singletons alive across reloads; edited config does not take effect until a full restart. Always prefer --watch for development to ensure clean module reloads.

example.title: package.json scripts for Bun

section.name: bunfig-toml

rule.statement: Set frozen-lockfile = true under [install] in bunfig.toml. Enable coverage under [test].
rule.rationale: Frozen lockfile in CI detects lockfile drift, identical to npm ci behavior.

section.reference: bunfig.toml with CI and test settings.

rule.statement: Set moduleResolution to "bundler" in tsconfig.json. This is required for Bun's native module resolution.
rule.rationale: Bun handles modern resolution natively; "bundler" mode enables Bun-specific path resolution without ts-node shims.

section.name: bun-serve

rule.statement: Use Bun.serve (or a framework like Hono on top of it) for HTTP servers. Do not use node:http when running on Bun.
rule.rationale: Bun.serve is measurably faster than node:http for I/O-heavy routes due to JavaScriptCore optimizations. The API is web standard — takes a Request, returns a Response, making it compatible with edge runtimes and HTTP specs.

section.p: Bun.serve accepts a configuration object with port, fetch handler, and optional error handler. The fetch handler receives a standard Web API Request object and must return a Response or Promise<Response>. This makes Bun servers trivial to port to edge functions or standard Web APIs.

example.title: Bun.serve basic usage

section.reference: Full Hono server with middleware, Zod validation, JWT routes.

rule.statement: Use Bun.file() for static reads — it is lazy-loaded and faster than fs.readFileSync.
rule.rationale: Bun.file returns a lazy Blob; data is only read when text()/json()/arrayBuffer() is called.

example.title: Bun.file usage

rule.statement: Wrap Bun.env access in a typed validation function using Zod. Never access Bun.env directly in business logic.
rule.rationale: Bun.env values are strings; Zod coercion handles number/boolean conversion and provides helpful missing-var errors at startup.

example.title: Typed environment with Zod

rule.statement: Use Bun.password.hash / Bun.password.verify for password hashing. Do not import bcrypt.
rule.rationale: Bun.password uses bcrypt or argon2 natively; no npm dependency needed.

example.title: Bun.password usage
example.code: const hash = await Bun.password.hash(password) const valid = await Bun.password.verify(password, hash)

rule.statement: Use describe/it/expect from bun:test. Avoid jest.mock — use mock.module() for ESM mocking in Bun.
rule.rationale: bun:test is Jest-compatible at the describe/it/expect level but ESM module mocking uses a different API.

antipattern.description: Running npm install after bun install, creating both package-lock.json and bun.lockb
antipattern.reason: package-lock.json takes precedence in some tools, causing non-deterministic dependency resolution. Pick one package manager and lock to it.

rule.statement: Use --frozen-lockfile in all CI bun install calls. Commit bun.lockb. Do not run npm/yarn/pnpm in the same project.
rule.rationale: Frozen lockfile in CI detects drift immediately rather than silently updating dependencies.

antipattern.description: Importing node:cluster, node:vm, or process.binding() — these differ or fail under Bun
antipattern.reason: Bun's Node compat is >95% but not 100%. These specific APIs have known gaps; use Bun.spawn or single-process multi-worker alternatives.

antipattern.description: Using child_process.exec instead of Bun's $ shell API or Bun.spawn
antipattern.reason: Bun provides $ (shell) and Bun.spawn as native alternatives with better ergonomics.

example.title: Bun shell API

antipattern.description: Using Bun.file() or Bun.serve() in a package consumed by both Bun and Node apps
antipattern.reason: Node import crashes with "Bun is not defined". Restrict Bun-native APIs to entry points or dev tooling; use isomorphic alternatives in shared packages.

antipattern.description: Writing Bun.serve for a Cloudflare Workers deploy target
antipattern.reason: Workers run on V8 isolates, not Bun. Workers expect export default { fetch }. Use bun build --target=browser for Workers.
--hot persists module state across reloads (DB pools, singletons). After config changes, old instances remain alive. Use --watch for dev; deploy a built bundle for prod.

warning.p: Node compat surprises. node:vm, node:cluster, parts of node:tls (e.g., custom cipher) work differently. Symptom: prod-style libs throw on Bun.
NPM packages with postinstall native builds sometimes fail under Bun's installer. Workarounds: bun pm trust, or fall back to npm for that step.
bun.lockb is binary. Conflicts in Git can't be resolved manually; use bun install again. Some teams prefer bun install --frozen-lockfile + JSON lockfile (v1.1+ supports --save-text-lockfile).
Test runner gaps. bun test lacks Jest's full snapshot mocking parity (less mature jest.mock). Mocking ESM modules differs.
Hot reload via --hot keeps process alive but persists module state across reloads — not always desired. --watch restarts cleanly.
Bundler quirks. bun build --target bun produces Bun-only output; deploying that to Node runs but loses Bun-specific imports (Bun.serve, Bun.file).
Logging differences. Bun streams stdout differently; CI log buffers may flush late. process.stdout.write works but flush behavior varies.
Memory profile. Bun's JavaScriptCore engine has different GC behavior than V8; high-allocation workloads behave differently.
Edge-deploy mismatch. bun dev on Mac, node on prod Vercel — Bun.env, Bun.file calls crash. Force isomorphic patterns or restrict Bun-only APIs to dev tooling.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Drive Bun work in 4 stages: (1) a runtime-detector subagent inspects target deploy environment (Vercel / Render / VPS / Docker / edge) and decides whether code can use Bun-native APIs or must stay isomorphic-Node; (2) an install-and-bootstrap subagent runs bun init / bun add and pins Bun version in package.json packageManager field + Dockerfile; (3) a server-author subagent generates Hono/Elysia routes via Bun.serve; (4) a test-author subagent writes bun test specs, exclusively using Jest-compatible API (describe/it/expect) to keep portability. Always pin Bun version (oven-sh/setup-bun@v2 with bun-version) in CI; LLMs default to "latest" which breaks builds across teams.
section.note: Recommended subagents: - faion-sdd-executor-agent — quality gate runs bun install --frozen-lockfile && bun run typecheck && bun test && bun build. - bun-compat-agent (worth creating): scans for Node-only APIs (node:cluster, node:vm, process.binding) and reports compatibility risk before migration. - bun-perf-agent (worth creating): runs bun --bun run vs node benchmarks (start time, RSS, RPS) and emits a comparison table. - lockfile-agent (worth creating): converts between package-lock.json ↔ bun.lockb and validates dep versions match. - dockerfile-agent that emits a multi-stage Bun Dockerfile pinned to oven/bun:1.1.x-alpine. - password-scrubber-agent — scrub .env, bunfig.toml, fixture credentials before commit.

rule.statement: Prompt pattern for server scaffold: "You are a Bun 1.1.x + Hono engineer. Generate src/index.ts: Hono app with routes: GET /health, POST /api/users; Validate body with zod (UserCreateSchema); Use Bun.serve via Hono adapter, port from process.env.PORT; Logging: console.info {method, path, status, latencyMs}; Tests in src/index.test.ts using bun:test. Do NOT import 'node:cluster' or 'node:worker_threads'. Run: bun install && bun test && bun --watch run src/index.ts".

Prompt pattern for compat audit: "Scan src/ for Node-only API usage that breaks under Bun: node:cluster, node:vm, node:async_hooks (custom hooks), process.binding(*), native packages requiring node-gyp. Output: file:line, API, risk (high/med/low), workaround."

section.name: cli-tools
section.note: Bun CLI Tools: - bun — Runtime, package manager, test runner, bundler (https://bun.sh) - bun init — Scaffold new project (bundled) - bun install — npm-compat installer (10-30x faster) (bundled) - bun add / bun remove — Manage deps (bundled) - bun run — Run scripts / TS files directly (bundled) - bun test — Jest-compatible test runner (https://bun.sh/docs/cli/test) - bun build — Bundler with --target=bun|node|browser (https://bun.sh/docs/bundler) - bun pm trust — Allow lifecycle scripts (postinstall) (https://bun.sh/docs/cli/pm) - bun --hot — Hot reload (persist state) (bundled) - bun --watch — Watch + restart (bundled) - bun upgrade — Update Bun itself (bundled)

template.purpose: Project manifest with scripts, packageManager pin, Hono + Zod deps.
Bun configuration: frozen lockfile in CI, test coverage, build minify.
Hono server with middleware, Zod validation, JWT-protected routes.
Multi-stage Bun Dockerfile pinned to oven/bun:1.1.x-alpine.
